name: Pyramids of Giza
summary: The Pyramids of Giza are the last surviving wonder of the ancient world, built during Egypt's Fourth Dynasty around 2560 BC. The Great Pyramid of Khufu stood as the tallest man-made structure on Earth for over 3,800 years. These monumental tombs continue to inspire awe as testaments to the engineering brilliance of ancient Egyptian civilization.

timeline.title: Construction of the Great Pyramid
timeline.description: Pharaoh Khufu commissions the Great Pyramid, the largest of the three Giza pyramids. Standing at 146.6 meters, it was built by an estimated workforce of 20,000 to 30,000 laborers over a period of approximately 20 years. The pyramid was constructed using an estimated 2.3 million limestone blocks, each weighing an average of 2.5 tons.

timeline.title: Completion of the Sphinx
timeline.description: The Great Sphinx of Giza is carved from a single ridge of limestone, likely during the reign of Pharaoh Khafre. Measuring 73 meters long and 20 meters high, it is one of the oldest and largest monolithic statues in the world. The Sphinx is believed to bear the face of Khafre himself, serving as a guardian of the Giza plateau.

timeline.title: Alexander the Great Visits Giza
timeline.description: After conquering Egypt, Alexander the Great visits the Pyramids of Giza and is deeply impressed by their grandeur. His visit marks the beginning of Greek fascination with Egyptian monuments. During the Ptolemaic period that follows, Greek scholars such as Herodotus document the pyramids, helping to spread their fame throughout the Mediterranean world.

timeline.title: Napoleon's Egyptian Expedition
timeline.description: Napoleon Bonaparte leads a military and scientific expedition to Egypt, bringing along 167 scholars, scientists, and artists to study the ancient monuments. His team conducts the first systematic surveys of the pyramids and discovers the Rosetta Stone. Napoleon reportedly tells his troops at the foot of the pyramids: 'Soldiers, from the summit of these pyramids, forty centuries look down upon you.'

timeline.title: Howard Vyse's Explosive Excavation
timeline.description: British colonel Richard William Howard Vyse uses gunpowder to blast his way into the Great Pyramid in search of hidden chambers. While his methods are destructive, he discovers the name of Khufu inscribed in red ochre in construction chambers above the King's Chamber, providing key evidence of the pyramid's builder. His work sparks both scientific interest and controversy over archaeological ethics.

timeline.title: Discovery of Khufu's Solar Boat
timeline.description: A sealed pit containing a dismantled cedarwood boat measuring 43.6 meters in length is discovered at the base of the Great Pyramid. The Khufu Ship, as it becomes known, is one of the oldest intact vessels ever found. It is believed to have been built for Pharaoh Khufu's use in the afterlife and provides invaluable insight into ancient Egyptian shipbuilding techniques.

timeline.title: UNESCO World Heritage Designation
timeline.description: The Giza pyramid complex, along with other pyramid sites at Memphis and its necropolis, is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This designation recognizes the pyramids as masterpieces of human creative genius and one of the most important archaeological sites on Earth. Conservation efforts intensify to protect the monuments from urban encroachment, pollution, and the effects of mass tourism.
